content.text:
I've got the lay of the land. Here's the picture:

## Planetfall — a 3D browser game that teaches the Entire checkpoint workflow

**What it is.** A Three.js + Vite game (no build assets — everything procedural) where a stranded astronaut restores their ship AI's lost memory. The fiction maps directly onto real Entire commands: restoring the ship's memory *is* the checkpoint workflow. The repo started life as `signalkit`, a Python telemetry CLI, and pivoted on June 5.

**The three-level arc** (all built, as of yesterday):

1. **First Memories** (`islandView.js`) — Diner Dash-style loop: walk to a glowing memory, `git add`, `git commit` (freezes it in ice), then accept Entire's `[y/n]` checkpoint-link offer to actually bank it. Finish with `entire checkpoint list` before a 45-second clock melts everything. Teaching: *freeze keeps it; checkpoint banks it.*
2. **The Drone Bay** (`droneBayView.js`) — five subagents repair five systems in parallel while you can only be in one place. Each finished job seals under a checkpoint; a pre-filled `entire checkpoint explain` shows what the drone actually did (they improvise — a bent dish comes back as a Signal Spire), then `entire dispatch` files the day's report.
3. **Launch Clearance** (`launchView.js`) — the cockpit finale, no walking. The ship AI asks five questions only the record can answer; you pick a tool from a rotating multiple-choice menu (commands and skills, mostly all-valid with the occasional dead end), confirm answers, fill the launch code, and lift off.

There's also a **shelved fourth level**, The Archive (`archiveView.js`, `?view=archive`) — a `checkpoint search` level built then pulled after a playtest, kept playable for later reuse.

**Architecture in one breath:** `main.js` is the view manager (renderer, crossfades, level progression); each level is a self-contained view module with `enter()/exit()/onComplete`; shared bits live in `memoryProps.js` (ice/beams/checkpoint ids), `firstPerson.js` (WASD walker), `overhead.js` (M-key map), and the procedural stack (`planet.js`, `terrain.js`, `noise.js`, `atmosphere.js`, `ring.js`). Dev shortcuts: `?view=island`, `?level=2`, `?level=3`, `?view=archive`.

**Current state:** working tree is clean against `main` — the only untracked thing is a `.codex/` directory (hooks config + an `entire-search` agent definition, from June 5). The most recent commits wrapped up Level 3, chained wins between levels, and refreshed the README to cover the full game. PLAN.md is meticulous — it records not just decisions but rejected designs (the corruption level, "The Other Ship", graphics overhauls) so they don't get re-trodden.

The game is feature-complete across all three levels; PLAN.md flags Level 3's name as a placeholder "pending playtest." Want me to run it, or dig into anything specific?

---
name: reference-bug-bash-ux

https://bug-bash.github.com/ — GitHub Design's "Mona's Bug Bash", the game Rizel
cites as the simplicity bar (see [[level3-anomaly-design]]). Playtested headlessly
2026-06-11; key UX facts:

- Page = dark room + centered rounded-corner canvas styled like a CRT TV; zero
  instructions on the page itself. Theater/fullscreen corner buttons only.
- Arcade feel = `image-rendering: pixelated` + chromatic aberration (RGB fringe)
  + scanlines + vignette/bezel + grey "phosphor" scene fades. Cheap post effects,
  not asset-heavy.
- Onboarding: title menu (Start/Options/Leaderboard/Credits) → 4 one-line
  dialogue beats (Space to advance) that ARE the story → goal in one sentence
  ("Help Mona squash the bugs before time runs out!") → controls in one
  parenthetical line → lights on, Time: 60, play. ~15 seconds total.
- One UI channel (bottom dialogue box) carries story+goal+controls; HUD is
  hearts + score + countdown, all self-explanatory arcade grammar.

Relevant to Planetfall: it front-loads far less text than our briefings; the
TV-bezel treatment would be cheap if Rizel ever wants it (but see
[[graphics-overhaul-rejected]] — don't add unasked).

content.text: That's the full picture — summary above stands, and I've saved the Bug Bash UX notes to memory for future design conversations. The three things it does that are most relevant to compare against Planetfall: the page-as-dark-room-with-a-TV framing, the ~15-second all-in-fiction onboarding (story, goal, and controls each get exactly one line in one dialogue box), and the cheap CRT post-effects (pixelated rendering, chromatic fringe, scanlines, bezel vignette) doing all the genre-setting before a single word is read.

content.text:
I agree with your instincts, and I'd push them one step further: the thing that makes Bug Bash's onboarding work isn't *where* the text is, it's that the story, the goal, and the controls all arrive through **one character, one box, one line at a time**. We already have that character — the ship AI. It's the amnesiac in L1 and the questioner in L3, but right now it doesn't actually *talk* to you in between. Here's how I'd rank the ideas:

## 1. Ship-AI dialogue beats replace the briefing panels (do this first)

Our current briefings are paragraph-style panels with a CONTROLS section — the player reads a wall, then plays. Swap each one for 3–5 one-line beats advanced with Space, in the ship AI's voice, always ending with the same two-line shape: **one goal sentence, one controls line.** Sketch for L1:

> *"...hello? Someone's out there. I can't remember anything."*
> *"My memory is scattered across this island. I can see the pieces glowing."*
> *"Freeze them, then link them to me — frozen isn't saved. Saved is saved."*
> **"Bank all three memories before the clock hits 0:00."**
> *(WASD to walk · walk up to a glow and type what the terminal shows)*

This also fixes a structural thing for free: the ship AI gains personality level by level as its memory returns — confused in L1, businesslike in L2, fully itself asking questions in L3. The finale's "I can read the record. Protocol says you have to be the one to confirm it" lands much harder if you've watched this voice come back to life. And the win→`Enter` chaining we already built is the natural place for one connective story beat between levels.

## 2. A real title screen (your "landing page" idea — yes)

Right now you land in orbit with no ceremony. Give it the arcade boot: the orbit view already running as the backdrop (exactly like Bug Bash's lab behind the logo), **PLANETFALL** in big type, and a tiny menu — `▸ Begin` / maybe `Levels` for the dev shortcuts. The story hook is one line under the logo: *"Your ship's AI has lost its memory."* Nothing else. The planet slowly rotating behind a title card is already a strong attract screen — we built it, we're just not using it as one.

## 3. TV framing — but as the *terminal's* skin, not the page's

Here's where I'd deviate from Bug Bash. Their CRT treatment works because the whole game is pixel art — scanlines and chromatic fringe over our soft lavender 3D world would fight the look (and it's adjacent to the graphics-overhaul territory we decided to stay out of). But the *terminal* is diegetically a crashed ship's display — scanlines, a faint flicker, a curved-glass vignette on the terminal overlay and dialogue boxes would be cheap CSS, would make every text moment feel like ship hardware, and the world stays clean. The "arcade TV" feeling gets attached to the thing the player reads, which is exactly where you want their attention anyway.

If we ever host it on a real page, the Bug Bash page model (dark room, framed canvas, title + one footer line, zero instructions outside the screen) is the right call there too — but that's a distribution question, not a game change.

## What I'd skip

Pixelated rendering, hearts/score HUD, leaderboards — those are Bug Bash being an arcade brawler. Our HUD grammar (clock, meter, fleet panel) is already self-explanatory the way their hearts are.

My recommendation on order: **dialogue beats first** (it touches all three levels and is mostly copy + a reusable beat-box component), **title screen second**, **terminal CRT skin third** (pure polish, no design risk). Want me to draft the actual beat scripts for all three levels so you can react to the writing before we build anything?

# Planetfall

Planetfall is a 3D browser game about a stranded astronaut whose ship AI has
lost its memory. To get home, the player restores the ship's memory while
learning the real [Entire](https://docs.entire.io) checkpoint workflow — three
levels, one arc:

1. **First Memories** — bank your own work: commit it, then link the
   checkpoint. Freeze keeps it; checkpoint banks it.
2. **The Drone Bay** — five subagents repair the ship in parallel while you
   can only be in one place. `entire checkpoint explain` shows you what each
   one did; `entire dispatch` turns the day into a report.
3. **Launch Clearance** — the finale. From the pilot's chair, the ship's AI
   asks questions only the record can answer. Pick the tool — a command, or a
   skill that runs it for you — confirm the answers, complete the launch code,
   and lift off.

The thread through all three: work you didn't watch is never a mystery if it
was checkpointed. You weren't there — your checkpoints were.

## Run

```bash
npm install
npm run dev
```

Open <http://localhost:5173>.

Useful commands:

```bash
npm run build
npm run dev -- --host 127.0.0.1
```

Development shortcuts:

- `?view=island` — straight to Level 1.
- `?view=level2` (or `?level=2`) — Level 2 with Level 1 checkpoints preloaded.
- `?view=level3` (or `?level=3`) — Level 3, the cockpit finale.
- `?view=archive` — a shelved search level kept playable for later.

## How To Play

Orbit view:

- Drag to orbit the planet, scroll to zoom, click the island pin to land.

On the island (Levels 1–2):

- Click to capture the mouse; move with WASD or the arrow keys.
- `M` toggles a bird's-eye map (you can keep walking), `Esc` releases the
  mouse or closes finished terminal output, `B` returns to orbit.

**Level 1 — First Memories.** Walk to a glowing memory; the ship terminal
opens. Type `git add`, then `git commit` to freeze it in ice. When Entire
offers to link the commit, press `y` — only linked checkpoints fill the ship
memory meter. Bank all three, then run `entire checkpoint list` before the
countdown hits `0:00`.

**Level 2 — The Drone Bay.** Five systems are dark and the clock can't be
beaten alone. Press `E` at a broken system and a subagent takes the job while
you move on — running all five at once is the whole point. When a beam turns
ice-blue, walk up: `entire checkpoint explain` comes pre-filled; press `Enter`,
read what the subagent actually did, then ADD TO SHIP (`Y`). After the fifth
accept, type `entire dispatch` to file the day's report.

**Level 3 — Launch Clearance.** No walking — you're in the pilot's chair, and
the launch computer won't arm on work nobody can account for. The ship asks
five questions about work you never watched. Pick a way to look each one up
(`1`–`3`): a raw command, a skill that runs it for you, or — occasionally — a
dead end that costs only seconds. The answer lands on screen; confirm it to
lock a launch-code segment. Five segments, then `entire dispatch` files the
flight log: ignition, fireworks, liftoff.

Winning a level offers `Enter` to carry you straight into the next. Failing
only ever offers `R` to retry — the clock is the only enemy.

## Current Features

- Procedural lavender ocean planet with metallic gold islands, atmosphere,
  clouds, stars, and a Saturn-style ring.
- Orbit-to-island flow with a tracked landing pin and crossfade transitions;
  wins chain levels directly with `Enter`.
- Walkable first-person island terrain, plus a bird's-eye map toggle.
- Diegetic terminals that accept the real workflow: `git add`, `git commit`,
  checkpoint linking, `entire checkpoint list` / `explain` / `search`, and
  `entire dispatch` — pre-filled where typing ids would be friction.
- Five worker subagents that improvise upgrades in parallel (a bent dish comes
  back as a signal spire), each job sealed under a checkpoint to review.
- A cockpit finale: rotating tool menus (commands and skills as different
  lenses on the same record), answer confirms, a filling launch code, and a
  3-2-1 ignition with fireworks on the climb out.
- Ice-block commit metaphor, checkpoint record cards, and in-world
  `Entire-Checkpoint: <id>` trailer labels matching the real shape.
- One countdown per level with urgent/critical clock states and a panic sky;
  fail screens with instant retry.
- Local background music with play, mute, and volume controls.

## How It's Built

- **Three.js + Vite** power the app.
- `index.html` defines the canvas, per-level HUD blocks, terminals, briefings,
  countdowns, win/fail screens, loader, and audio controls.
- `src/main.js` owns the renderer, view switching and level progression,
  resizing, and the render loop.
- `src/planetView.js` builds the orbit scene: planet, clouds, atmosphere,
  ring, stars, controls, and the landable island pin.
- `src/islandView.js` is Level 1: terrain, memory artifacts, the terminal
  state machine, countdown, checkpoint logic, failure, and reset.
- `src/droneBayView.js` is Level 2: five broken systems, five subagents,
  pre-filled explain reviews, and the dispatch finish line.
- `src/launchView.js` is Level 3: the cockpit rig, question/tool/answer
  console, launch code, and the liftoff sequence.
- `src/archiveView.js` is the shelved search level, kept playable.
- `src/overhead.js` adds the bird's-eye map; `src/firstPerson.js` wraps
  `PointerLockControls` for terrain-glued walking.
- `src/memoryProps.js` holds the shared beam/ice/checkpoint-id props;
  `src/debris.js` defines the memory artifacts.
- `src/terrain.js`, `src/planet.js`, `src/noise.js`, `src/atmosphere.js`, and
  `src/ring.js` generate the procedural terrain and planet visuals.

Most visual assets are generated at runtime; the music is bundled locally in
`public/audio/asteroid-circuit.mp3`.

## Project History

This repo started as `signalkit`, a Python telemetry CLI. On June 5, 2026 it
pivoted into Planetfall: a procedural Three.js game for teaching Entire through
play. The old code and the design evolution are recoverable through git history
and Entire checkpoints.

content.text:
The title screen is built and verified. Here's what's in place:

**What it does**
- On a clean boot the game now opens to an arcade menu over the live orbit view — the rotating planet is the attract backdrop, Bug Bash style. Big **PLANETFALL** logo, the one-line hook *"YOUR SHIP'S AI HAS LOST ITS MEMORY"*, then **START GAME / OPTIONS**.
- **Options → Controls** shows the full key list grouped by context (In Orbit / On the Island / In the Cockpit), using the same key-chip styling as the briefings.
- **Options → Sound** has exactly what you asked for: **MUSIC ON/OFF** and a **VOLUME** slider. Both are wired *through* the existing in-game audio panel, so the title menu and the corner widget can never disagree — I verified a volume change in the menu lands on the music element, the in-game slider, and the menu's own display simultaneously.
- Navigation is full arcade grammar: ↑/↓ move the ▸ cursor, Enter selects, Esc backs out, ←/→ nudge the volume row — and mouse hover/click works everywhere too.
- The dev shortcuts (`?view=`, `?level=`) skip it, and the in-game chrome (small logo, hint, audio panel, landing pin) stays hidden until you press start. Nice side effect: the first menu press is the browser gesture that unblocks music autoplay, so "press start" genuinely starts the soundtrack.

**Where it lives:** `src/titleScreen.js` (new), markup in `index.html`, styles in `style.css`, two-line hookup in `main.js`. README and PLAN.md are updated — PLAN also records the two Bug Bash ideas we discussed but haven't built yet (ship-AI dialogue beats, terminal-scoped CRT skin) so they don't get lost.

I tested the whole flow in headless Chrome: boot → title → Options → Controls → back → Sound → music off (label flips, audio pauses) → volume nudges → Esc out → START GAME → title fades, orbit HUD returns. Screenshots are at `/tmp/ts-1-title.png` through `/tmp/ts-7-started.png` if you want to look before playing it yourself with `npm run dev`.

Nothing is committed yet — say the word if you want it on `main`.
